coredata.dc:title: Direct laser writing carbonization based on internal-reflection setup for fabricating shape-tailorable and sealed in-situ carbon features
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2021 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Jiang, Zhufeng
Luo, Jiangjiang
Yao, Jingwen
Xu, Chang
Liu, Aishuang
Yao, Yanbo
Liu, Tao
coredata.dc:description: The recently-developed direct laser writing carbonization method based on an internal-reflection setup (IR-DLWc) reported in our works is a novel and efficient approach that enables a controllable fabrication of carbon features sealed in the interior of polyimide film in-situ, which may lead to the flexible and versatile preparation of carbon-based functional devices and wearable electronics. To fully exploit this useful method, in the present study, we combine the experimental and modeling/simulation studies to elucidate the unique photothermal and thermomechanical processes and to reveal the processing-structure relationship of this novel technique. The processing conditions that allow for the sealing of the carbon dot created by IR-DLWc were identified; and the empirical relation between the size of the carbon dots and the laser processing conditions were established. Moreover, it was found that the thermal–mechanical stress induced by the trapped pyrolysis gas is able to deform the PI matrix surrounding a carbon dot and to cause polymer chain reorientation. This phenomenon was further utilized to tailor the shape of carbon features for readily fabricating highly-sensitive piezoresistive sensors, which has been successfully demonstrated for the underwater use in mapping the ultrasound field.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Direct laser writing carbonization
Carbon features sealed in-situ
Photo-thermal modeling
Piezoresistive sensor
Ultrasound mapping

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication of the carbon features sealed in-situ via IR-DLWc
Characterization of carbon dots created by IR-DLWc
Piezoresistivity evaluation for the IR-DLWc created carbon-dot linear assembly and its application demonstration for underwater ultrasound mapping
FEA modeling of the photo-thermal process in the fabrication of the carbon dots sealed in-situ by IR-DLWc
Fabrication and characterization of the carbon dots sealed in-situ by IR-DLWc
FEA photothermal modeling of the IR-DLWc process and its comparison with the experimental results
Trapping of pyrolysis gas in IR-DLWc and its induced polymer chain reorientation
In-situ tailoring the shape of carbon dots in IR-DLWc and its use for making sensitive piezoresistive sensors

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China [ NSFC 22105141 , NSFC51673140 ]; Startup funds provided by Soochow University [ Q410900116 ]; State and Local Joint Engineering Laboratory for Novel Functional Polymeric Materials;and Priority Academic Program Development (PAPD) of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) A schematic diagram of the IR-DLWc process and the representative temperature distribution by FEA simulation (Inset: optical micrograph of the cross-section of the carbon dot sealed-situ prepared by IR-DLWc). (b) A schematic diagram of the conventional DLWc process and the representative temperature distribution by FEA simulation (Inset: optical micrograph of the cross-section of the protruded carbon feature prepared by DLWc). Mechanical stability evaluation of the carbon dot prepared by IR-DLWc (c) and the conventional DLWc (d) through peeling test with an adhesive tape.

(a) Optical micrographs of carbon dots fabricated by IR-DLWc at (a) fixed laser power and varied irradiation duration and (b) fixed irradiation duration and varied laser power. All scale bars are 200 μm. Dependence of the diameter of the carbon dot on (c) the laser irradiation duration at different laser powers and (d) the laser power level at different irradiation durations.

(a) Optical (Upper) and SEM (Lower) micrographs of the cross-section of the carbon dots fabricated by IR-DLWc at 1.8 W and varied irradiation durations. All scale bars are 100 μm. The dependence of the residual thickness of PI layer at the side of PI/Glass (b) and ZnSe/PI (c) on the irradiation duration at a fixed laser power of 1.8 W. (d) Raman spectra acquired at the center region for the carbon dots in (a). (e) Raman spectra mapped from the center to the edge for the carbon dot fabricated at 1.8 W and 40 msec.

(a) Axisymmetric geometrical model for the photothermal FEA modeling of IR-DLWc process. (b) 3D temperature distribution in the PI film being irradiated at 1.8 W for 500 msec in IR-DLWc. (c) Comparison of the laser power dependence of the diameter of the carbon dot at varied irradiation duration determined by experimental measurements (unfilled symbols), FEA modeling (filled symbols), and the empirical correlation (Eq. (3), dashed line). (d) Comparison of the experimentally determined and the Eq. (3) predicted diameter values for the carbon dot created by different combinations of laser power and irradiation duration.

(a) Optical/SEM micrographs to show the bulge formation/collapse surrounding the carbon dots sealed in-situ by IR-DLWc. (b) Schematic setup and selected optical images for examining the reversible height change of the bulge under weights. (c) Dependence of the bulge height on the applied weights upon loading and unloading. The carbon dot sealed-situ was prepared by IR-DLWc at 1.6 W and 100 msec.

(a) Polarized optical microscopic images to show the polymer chain orientation states around the carbon dot fabricated by IR-DLWc at 1.8 W and 40 msec. P: polarization direction of polarizer; A: polarization direction of analyzer; MD: machine direction of the parent PI film. Φ: Angle between MD and P. (b) Proposed polymer chain orientation states around the carbon dot fabricated by IR-DLWc.

(a) Tailoring the shape of carbon dot by varying the dot-to-dot distance. Fabrication conditions: A first (1.8 W/20 msec) and then B (1.8 W/40 msec). (b) Tailoring the shape of carbon dot by varying the irradiation duration. Fabrication conditions: C first (1.8 W/40 msec) and then D (1.8 W/varied irradiation duration as indicated); dot-to-dot distance: 280 μm. (c) Optical micrograph of the dot-dot-contact line sensor fabricated by IR-DLWc at 1.8 W/40 msec with dot-to-dot distance of 230 μm. (d) Tensile strain-dependent relative resistance change of the dot-dot-contact line sensor in (c). (e) Cyclic tensile test (200 Cycles at 0.0125 Hz with strain amplitude of 0.2%) for examining the durability of the dot-dot-contact line sensor in (c). (f) Prototype of the ultrasonic sensor based on the dot-dot-contact line fabricated by IR-DLWc and its use for underwater mapping of the ultrasound field of a bath sonication cleaner (Frequency 33.2 kHz).

head.ce:title: Direct laser writing carbonization based on internal-reflection setup for fabricating shape-tailorable and sealed in-situ carbon features

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering and Materials Science, Soochow University, Soochow 215123, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering and Materials Science, Soochow University, Soochow 215123, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The recently-developed direct laser writing carbonization method based on an internal-reflection setup (IR-DLWc) reported in our works is a novel and efficient approach that enables a controllable fabrication of carbon features sealed in the interior of polyimide film in-situ, which may lead to the flexible and versatile preparation of carbon-based functional devices and wearable electronics. To fully exploit this useful method, in the present study, we combine the experimental and modeling/simulation studies to elucidate the unique photothermal and thermomechanical processes and to reveal the processing-structure relationship of this novel technique. The processing conditions that allow for the sealing of the carbon dot created by IR-DLWc were identified; and the empirical relation between the size of the carbon dots and the laser processing conditions were established. Moreover, it was found that the thermal–mechanical stress induced by the trapped pyrolysis gas is able to deform the PI matrix surrounding a carbon dot and to cause polymer chain reorientation. This phenomenon was further utilized to tailor the shape of carbon features for readily fabricating highly-sensitive piezoresistive sensors, which has been successfully demonstrated for the underwater use in mapping the ultrasound field.

ce:section.ce:para: The direct laser writing carbonization (DLWc) of polymers such as polyimide receives great attention recently as a viable technique for producing functional carbon materials [1–3] for a variety of promising applications, e.g. all-solid flexible supercapacitor [4,5], flexible and wearable strain sensor [6,7] and humidity sensor [8], artificial throat [9], flexible electrodes [10], electrocatalytic reactor [11], and heating elements [12]. In contrast to the traditional direct-heating induced polymer pyrolysis/carbonization method, the DLWc technique enables the design and fabrication of the carbonized features/patterns and their arrays with great flexibility and simplicity without the requirement for ink formulation, meanwhile possessing the characteristics of environmental friendliness, high precision and high controllability [5]. The past research on the operation of DLWc mainly relied on an external reflection configuration, in which the laser beam of different wavelength [10,13–23] is incident from air onto the carbonizable polymers, including polyimide [24], polysiloxane [25], cellulose paper [13], wood [26], even bread and potato [27]. In such a process, the transfer of the photothermally induced heat is controlled by the conduction through the polymer matrix and the convection with the surrounding air [24] or tailored with a quartz substrate when very thin polymeric film is to be carbonized [28]. Owing to the poor thermal conductivity of air and the associated inefficient heat transfer through the natural convection, the photothermally generated heat in the external reflection DLWc is located at the top surface of the polymer being processed. As a consequence, it normally causes the finally formed carbonized or graphene-like porous features to protrude above the surface of the polymer substrate [15], which, if without further packaging protection, can be damaged easily and makes the reliability of such fabricated device a serious issue. In addition, during the typical external reflection DLWc process, the pyrolysis/carbonization region is exposed to the ambient air, which may introduce the undesired oxidation reaction to deteriorate the electrical property of the carbon features. To overcome the above-mentioned disadvantages of the external reflection DLWc, the DLWc based on an internal-reflection setup (IR-DLWc) was proposed recently [1], which could potentially promote the highly-promising laser carbonization technology for robust fabricating low-cost, multi-functional, high-performance, high-stability and environmentally friendly carbon-based devices. Instead of using the air, the IR-DLWc method relies on a transparent optical window as the incident medium for the laser beam to perform the DLWc process. As such, the distribution of the photothermally generated heat source can be tailored by the conduction path through the intimate contact of the optical window with the polymer substrate, which allows for the confinement of the pyrolysis and carbonization reaction and the finally formed carbon features in the interior of the polymer film being processed. It is noted that a variety of works have been reported regarding using short-wavelength, tight-focus and pulsed laser to in-situ create/modify 3D miniaturized structures in a bulk material [29–34]. In such processes, the strong non-linear absorption plays a major role to cause the dielectric breakdown and ablation of the material being processed, which is not desired in DLWc since the ablation induced C-C bond breakage would lead to low yield of carbon residuals. Instead, in DLWc, one often uses a long-wavelength CO2 laser as the heat source to induce the pyrolysis/carbonization reaction of different polymer precursors, including PI. Given its very low photon energy of CO2 laser, there is no occurrence of the electronic excitation but rather the dissipation of lattice or chemical bond vibration induced photo-thermal effect, which coupled with the heat-transfer/diffusion and the pyrolysis/carbonization reaction of the laser irradiated polymer to form the desired carbon structures.

The IR-DLWc method presents a tremendous improvement over the conventional DLWc technique in that it makes the carbon features being sealed in-situ and is able to avoid the undesired oxidation side-reaction [35–37] during the pyrolysis and carbonization. The similar effect can be achieved by performing the conventional DWLc in an inert atmosphere, such as argon [35,36]. Moreover, the in-situ sealed structures by IR-DLWc could significantly improve the stability of the mechanical and electrical properties of the finally formed carbon features without involving additional packaging processes. We previously focused on investigating the piezoresistive behavior of the carbon line features fabricated by IR-DLWc technique, and demonstrated their promising use as sensitive and self-protected wearable sensors in tactile sensing, swallowing/breathing/pulsing monitoring and finger gesture recognition [1]. Nevertheless, up-to-date, there is a lack of systematic and in-depth study to understand the detailed photo-thermal and thermal–mechanical processes involved in IR-DLWc; and it is unclear what are the conditions to enable the complete sealing of the carbon features created by this process. Herein, we are aiming at revealing the unique photothermally induced physical, chemical and mechanical processes occurred in IR-DLWc and attempting to gain a better understanding of the related processing-structure relationship involved in this novel method. With the dot features created in IR-DLWc as the subject, the experimental studies in combination with FEA simulation allow us to be able to identify the processing conditions of IR-DLWc that enable the successful fabrication of the carbon features sealed in-situ and establish an empirical correlation for their size dependence on the laser processing parameters. Moreover, in contrast to the conventional external-reflection based DLWc, the pyrolysis gases created in IR-DLWc are trapped in the interior of the PI film. This causes an internal pressure accumulation to produce the mechanical stresses that can deform the local PI matrix surrounding a given carbon feature and cause the polymer chain reorientation. Polarized optical microscopic investigation was applied to vividly reveal this interesting phenomenon. In addition, the mechanical stresses created by the trapped pyrolysis gas in the IR-DLWc have been demonstrated to be utilized for tailoring the carbon dot shape to create a linear assembly of such deformed carbon dots, which possesses high piezoresistive sensitivity and can be used underwater for mapping the ultrasound field. In view of the technical significance for facile, cost-effective and scalable manufacturing of patternable, shape-tailorable carbon features sealed in-situ, the IR-DLWc technique expects to be highly useful in many different fields, such as flexible electronics, optoelectronics, chemical and biological sensing, energy storage, electrocatalysis, separation and filtration, and soft robotics.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of the carbon features sealed in-situ via IR-DLWc
ce:section.ce:para: A laser engraving and cutting system (SCE4030, Wuhan Sunic Photoelectricity Equipment Manufacture Co., Ltd.) equipped with a Synrad® 10 W CO2 laser of wavelength 10.6 μm was used to perform IR-DLWc operation. The setup of IR-DLWc is schematically shown in Fig. 1 a, where a poly(pyromellitic dianhydride-co-4,4′-oxydianiline) (PMDA/ODA) type PI film (length × width × thickness = 35 mm × 25 mm × 125 μm, Changzhou Gaoyuan Chemical Co. Ltd.) is tightly sandwiched between a ZnSe optical window (Diameter = 20 mm, Thickness = 2 mm, Chengdu Jinghengrui Photoelectric Inc.) and a piece of glass. On the laser entrance side, the ZnSe window is coated with antireflection coating. Before assembling the ZnSe/PI/Glass sandwiched structure, all elements were cleaned with ethanol and isopropanol. During the operation of IR-DLWc, the laser beam waist position was nominally set at 2625 μm below the interface of ZnSe/PI, and the laser power as well as the irradiation duration were varied respectively in a range of 1.0 W–1.8 W and 20 msec–500 msec.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of carbon dots created by IR-DLWc
ce:section.ce:para: The morphologies of the carbon dots fabricated at different conditions were observed with a polarized optical microscope (OM, MP41, Guangzhou Mingmei Optoelectronic Technology Co., Ltd.) and a scanning electron microscope (SEM, Hitachi SU8010). The latter instrument was also used to acquire the energy dispersive spectra (EDS) to determine the elemental compositions of the carbon dots. A confocal Raman microscope in backscattering geometry with a 633 nm excitation laser (LabRamHR800, 50× objective, laser beam size < 10 μm) was used to collect the Raman spectra of the carbon dots.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Piezoresistivity evaluation for the IR-DLWc created carbon-dot linear assembly and its application demonstration for underwater ultrasound mapping
ce:section.ce:para: The in-situ sealed carbon-dot linear assembly was prepared by consecutively writing a single dot each time in a straight line with dot-to-dot distance of 230 μm at the condition of laser power of 1.8 W and irradiation duration of 40 msec by IR-DLWc. Subsequently, the conventional DLWc process was applied (Power = 1.5 W, scanning speed = 10 mm/s) to this linear structure at the two pre-selected locations to remove the PI seal and expose the interior carbon structure. At these two locations, two copper wires were then inserted and glued with silver adhesives as electrical contacts for later electrical test use. A previously disclosed mechanical–electrical coupled test procedure [1,6] was implemented to evaluate the piezoresistive performance of the carbon-dot linear assembly via Q800 dynamic mechanical analyzer (DMA, TA instruments) and Keithley 3706A system switch/multimeter equipped with a 3721 dual 1 × 20 multiplexer card.

ce:section.ce:section-title: FEA modeling of the photo-thermal process in the fabrication of the carbon dots sealed in-situ by IR-DLWc
ce:section.ce:para:
By referring to our previous work [24], we established a COMSOL® FEA model to investigate the photothermal heating induced temperature rise behavior in a PI film when a Gaussian laser beam is incident to a ZnSe/PI/Glass three-layer structure in IR-DLWc. It should be mentioned that, when the Gaussian beam passes plane dielectric interfaces, its beam waist shifts to a different position [38–41]. As such, the nominally set waist location of the laser beam is modified due to the insertion of the ZnSe window and the beam size at a position z from ZnSe/PI interface would be changed accordingly. By taking account of this effect, the beam size inside the polyimide is given by:
(1)
w
( z )
=
λ
π
w 0
1
n
pi
z -
n
pi
E O F +
d
ZnSe
+
n
pi
d
ZnSe
n
ZnSe
2
+
π
2
n
pi
2
W 0
4
λ
2
where λ is the laser wavelength; w0 is the beam waist; EOF is the nominal waist location (distance of the nominal laser beam waist in air from the ZnSe/PI interface); dZnSe is the thickness of ZnSe window; and nPI and nZnSe are the refractive index of PI and ZnSe respectively. The derivation details of Eq. (1) can be found in the Supporting Information - S1.1. With assistance of Eq. (1), the photothermally generated heat source density in the PI film at the position (r, z) can then be written as [24]:
(2)
q
( r , z )
=
4 π κ
λ
( 1 - R )
2 P
w ( z )
2
e x p
- 2
r
2
w ( z )
2
e x p
-
4 π κ z
λ
where κ is the imaginary part of the complex refractive index of polyimide or its pyrolyzed/carbonized product; R is the reflectivity of the ZnSe window; P is the laser power. Other than the modification of the beam size and the corresponding heat source density mentioned above, the FEA modeling for IR-DLWc is similar to that of the conventional DLWc process previously disclosed [24]. The additional details about FEA modeling of the IR-DLWc process can be found in the Support Information - S1.2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication and characterization of the carbon dots sealed in-situ by IR-DLWc
ce:section.ce:para: The IR-DLWc method (Fig. 1a) is different from the conventional DLWc (Fig. 1b) in that the CO2 laser beam is incident from the high refractive index ZnSe window rather than air into the PI film to cause its pyrolysis/carbonization induced by photothermal heating. Due to the much higher thermal conductivity of ZnSe (18 Wm−1K−1) than air (0.026 Wm−1K−1), the extreme heat accumulated on the surface of the PI film as in the conventional DLWc [24] can be efficiently dissipated away through the conduction path by the ZnSe window. For this reason, the maximum temperature downshifts from the top surface of the PI in conventional DLWc (Fig. 1b) to its interior in IR-DLWc (Fig. 1a). Therefore, it may allow for thus fabricated carbon features to be sealed in the interior of the parent PI film. As a representative example, the inset of Fig. 1a shows the optical micrograph of the cross-section of an in-situ sealed carbon dot fabricated by IR-DLWc. For comparison, Fig. 1b shows the optical image of the cross-section of a carbon feature created by the conventional DLWc, which obviously protrudes above the surface of the parent PI film, a typical feature for DLWc. Due to its self-sealing or self-packaging character, the carbon dot fabricated by IR-DLWc shows excellent mechanical stability. This is evidenced by the peeling test with an adhesive tape as shown in Fig. 1c. In contrast, the protruding carbon dot fabricated by the conventional DLWc is completely damaged when subjected to the same peeling test (Fig. 1d).

In order to explore the laser processing conditions to seal the carbon dots inside of the parent PI film, we explored a wide range combinations of laser power (1 W–1.8 W) and irradiation duration (0–500 msec). It was found that when the power is below 1 W, no carbon dots can be created even if the irradiation duration exceeds several hundreds of milliseconds or even a few seconds. On the other hand, if the IR-DLWc was performed at a too high laser power (>1.8 W) and long irradiation duration, there is also a danger to damage/contaminate the ZnSe optical window. After identifying the appropriate laser processing conditions to seal the carbon dots, we investigated the dependence of its diameter on the laser power and the irradiation duration. As examples, Fig. 2 a shows the optical micrographs of the carbon dots created at a fixed laser power (1.5 W) and varied irradiation duration (20–500 msec). Fig. 2b shows a similar result but with irradiation duration fixed (500 msec) and varied laser power (1.0–1.8 W). As can be seen there, the diameter of the carbon dot in general increases with the laser power and the irradiation duration. The details of the dependence of the carbon dot diameter on the irradiation duration (at fixed laser power) and on the laser power (at fixed irradiation duration) are respectively summarized in Fig. 2c and 2d. It can be seen in Fig. 2c, at a fixed laser power above 1.1 W, the diameter of the carbon dot initially increases rapidly with the irradiation duration and then gradually levels off to reach a steady-state value. Similar trend can also be seen in Fig. 2d for the dependence of the carbon dot diameter on the laser power at fixed irradiation duration.

By fixing the laser power at 1.8 W, we further investigated the evolution of the internal structure of the carbon dots sealed in-situ with the laser irradiation duration. The cross-section views examined by both the optical and the scanning electron microscopy for such created carbon dots are shown in Fig. 3 a, which manifest the shape of the carbon dot like a discus. Moreover, as shown by the SEM images, they possess a relatively dense internal structure, which is different from the rich porous structures of the carbon dots fabricated by the conventional DLWc [24,42]. As compared to the conventional DLWc, the presence of the ZnSe window in IR-DLWc significantly modifies the temperature field distribution and the correspondingly induced pyrolysis/carbonization reaction. This is responsible for the observed discus-shaped carbon dots and their relatively dense internal structures. On the basis of the SEM images shown in Fig. 3a, we estimated the residual thickness of the PI layer surrounding the carbon dot at the side of PI/Glass (Fig. 3b) and ZnSe/PI (Fig. 3c). As expected, the residual thickness for both of the PI layers decreases with increasing the irradiation duration, a result due to the expansion of the pyrolysis/carbonization region. Raman scattering spectroscopy was applied to characterize the structures of the carbon dots obtained in Fig. 3a with the sampling region focused in the center of the carbon dot. At all irradiation duration conditions, the Raman spectra of the carbon dots (Fig. 3d) show the characteristics of disordered sp2 carbon structure[43], two broad and overlapped bands – one is the G-band at around 1595 cm−1 and another is the D-band at around 1350 cm−1, which is typical for the incomplete pyrolysis product of the polyimide at relative low temperature [44]. The EDS elemental analysis (Fig. S2) indicates that the carbon dots fabricated at 1.8 W and different irradiation durations all include a large fraction of nitrogen and oxygen, once again corroborating the incomplete pyrolysis of the polyimide in the IR-DLWc process.

The long-wavelength nature of CO2 laser makes its interaction with polymer precursor mainly through photo-thermal effect. Therefore, the formation of carbons from PI in IR-DLWc expects to follow the similar mechanisms as that in direct-heating process through a pyrolysis/carbonization reaction of the polymer precursor, albeit with different heating sources to induce such reactions. Polyimide derived carbon materials by direct heating have been studied more than several decades [45]. According to these studies, one may expect in DLWc or IR-DLWc process, the pyrolysis and carbonization of polyimide at elevated temperatures will also occur. As such, the planar aromatic structure units of the PMDA-ODA pyrolyzes at about 500–650 °C to release CO and CO2 in the meanwhile. Accompanying with such a pyrolysis reaction, the pyrolyzed product shows abrupt mass reduction and pronounced volume shrinkage. When the temperature is further increased above 800 °C, some additional gases, such as methane, hydrogen and nitrogen can also be released to eventually result in the carbon formation.

It has long been recognized that the surrounding gas plays a critical role in dictating the structures and the properties of polymer derived carbon in direct heating process induced pyrolysis/carbonization process [46]. In IR-DLWc of polyimide, the trapped pyrolysis gases expect to contain the similar components, such as CO2, CO, N2, HCN, etc. as those being generated in a directly-heating induced pyrolysis/carbonization process [45]. The presence of these gases in a small confinement at high temperature may lead to the modifications of the finally formed carbon structures, which requires further investigation. Our previous work [24] showed that there exists a very large temperature gradient across the diameter of the carbon dot fabricated by the conventional DLWc. This is also the case for the IR-DLWc process, as evidenced by the Raman spectra mapped across its diameter for the carbon dot created at 1.8 W and 40 msec (Fig. 3e), which evidently show the gradual disappearance of the characteristic G-band and D-band from the center to the edge of the carbon dot.

ce:section.ce:section-title: FEA photothermal modeling of the IR-DLWc process and its comparison with the experimental results
ce:section.ce:para:
As learned previously, the pyrolysis and carbonization of polyimide in the IR-DLWc process occur in the interior of the polyimide film, which makes it an extreme challenge for directly measuring the temperature evolution in-situ. To better understand the IR-DLWc process, we herein resort to FEA modeling and simulation to determine theoretically the transient temperature field in IR-DLWc. Fig. 4 (a) shows the axisymmetric geometrical model used in the FEA modeling work. According to the details described in Section 2 and the Supporting Information (S1) we calculated the transient temperature distribution for a ZnSe/PI/Glass three-layer structure when irradiated by a Gaussian laser beam at different laser power for varied durations. As an example, Fig. 4(b) shows the 3D temperature distribution of the PI film in IR-DLWc when irradiated at 1.8 W for 500 msec, where one can see the shape of the temperature field resembles the discus shape of the carbon dots created experimentally (Fig. 3a). The highest temperature achieved at this condition is ∼800 K, significantly lower than a few thousands of degrees achieved in the conventional DLWc process and even lower than the initial decomposition temperature 858 K of polyimide [24]. This is probably because, at some points due to structural and chemical defects or inhomogeneities, carbonization may start at lower temperature, infrared absorption, scattering and heating in the initially modified points may become much stronger, and may assist to carbonization in the neighboring regions, till the entire irradiated area becomes carbonized. As a consequence of low temperature pyrolysis, the pyrolysis/carbonization of the polyimide is incomplete to result in the carbon dot with a much higher contents of residual nitrogen and oxygen (Fig. S2) and the disordered carbon structure (Fig. 3d and 3e). For the conventional DLWc process, it was found that the initial decomposition temperature 858 K of the polyimide delineates the boundary of the carbon dot [24]. Upon comparing the Raman spectra acquired at the edge of the carbon dot in the IR-DLWc (Fig. 3e) with those reported previously for the conventional DLWc (Fig. 3 of [24]), one can find that the characteristic G-band and D-band for the sp2 carbons are completely disappeared in the former case but are clearly evident in the latter case. This observation suggests that the temperature value to delineate the boundary of the carbon dot in IR-DLWc should be much lower than 858 K. With the FEA modeling results for IR-DLWc, we found that, with a contour temperature of 768 K to delineate the boundary of the carbon dots, the experimentally measured diameter values can satisfactorily agree with those predicted by the FEA simulation, as shown in Fig. 4c. Moreover, within the experimental conditions being investigated, the dependence of the diameter of the carbon dot (D: μm) on the laser power (P: W) and the irradiation duration (t: msec) can be nicely described by the following empirical relation:
(3)
D
( P , t )
=
8087 ·
t
- 1.218
+ 123.5
× ln
ln
P
+ 375.3

Fig. 4d compares the experimentally determined diameter values of the carbon dots prepared at all laser conditions with the ones calculated by Eq. (3), the agreement is good. The empirical relation (Eq. (3)) is useful as a working tool for guiding the selection of appropriate laser processing conditions to achieve the carbon dots with the desired sizes.

In addition to the diameter of the carbon dot, we also relied on the FEA modeling results for the IR-DLWc to investigate the dependence of the residual thickness of the PI layer at both the sides of ZnSe/PI and PI/Glass on the laser power and the irradiation duration. Fig. S3 compares the FEA predicted results with those experimentally measured for the carbon dots prepared at 1.8 W and varied irradiation durations. Unlike the diameter of the carbon dots, there is a relatively large discrepancy on the residual thickness of the PI layer determined by the FEA modeling and the ones experimentally measured. The same situation occurs for the conventional DLWc process [24], in which the FEA modeling and the experimental measurement have a good agreement on the lateral size of the carbon dot but not on its dimension in the thickness direction. To overcome this deficiency, additional refining work in the future is necessary by considering the temporal and spatial changes of the optical constants of the pyrolyzed intermediate and final product of the PI, which has not been considered in the current photothermal FEA model for both the IR-DLWc and the conventional DLWc.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Trapping of pyrolysis gas in IR-DLWc and its induced polymer chain reorientation
ce:section.ce:para: In contrast to the conventional DLWc process, the pyrolyzed product of PI in the IR-DLWc is in-situ sealed in the interior of the parent PI film. Due to this fact, we can imagine that the pyrolysis gas produced in this process may also be trapped in a closed space, the wall of which is formed by the PI film surrounding the carbon dot. Since the volume of the PI precursor being pyrolyzed is much smaller than that of the produced pyrolysis gas [47,48], the pressure inside this closed space should be higher than the ambient pressure. As such, when the ZnSe/PI/Glass structure is disassembled after the completion of IR-DLWc process, one can see the expansion of this closed space. To examine this point, we prepared a series of carbon dots at 1.8 W and varied irradiation duration. After disassembling the ZnSe/PI/Glass structure, the free PI films processed at different laser conditions were imaged side-view as shown in Fig. 5 a, where each image is stitched by two parts: the left with the carbon dot region in focus and the right with the edge of the PI film in focus. For all samples except the one being irradiated for 100 msec, one can see a PI bulge elevated above the base of the parent PI film; and the height of the bulge increases with the irradiation duration. A closer look at the sample being irradiated for 100 msec by SEM reveals that it also possesses a collapsed bulge structure with a cracked PI ceiling, through which the porous structure of the carbon dot can be seen clearly. The results shown in Fig. 5a suggest that there exists pressurized pyrolysis gas being trapped inside the bulge structure. When the pressure is high enough, the correspondingly generated mechanical stress may cause the failure of the PI film that surrounds the bulge. To further confirm the pressurized pyrolysis gas being trapped in the closed space occupied by the carbon dot, we prepared the bulge structure by using IR-DLWc at 1.6 W for 100 msec and observed its height change upon loading and unloading different weights. Fig. 5b shows the schematic setup for this testing along with a few selected images on the bulge height at different weights. Fig. 5c summarizes the bulge height change at different weights during both loading and unloading process, where one can see that, during the loading process, the bulge height monotonically decreases with increasing the weight; upon unloading, the bulge height almost follows the same path to recover to its original state. The reversible deformation of the bulge structure created by IR-DLWc once again suggests the trap of pressurized pyrolysis gas in it, just like an inflated balloon.

As shown above, the trapped pyrolysis gas surrounding the carbon dot sealed in-situ is pressurized. Given this fact, we can infer that, during the process of IR-DLWc, the internal pressure in the laser irradiation region gradually increases with the proceeding of the pyrolysis of PI. Due to the build-up of the internal pressure, the PI matrix surrounding the pyrolyzed/carbonized region would be subjected to mechanical stresses, which is compression in the radial direction and tension in the circumferential direction, when using the laser beam as the axisymmetric reference axis. This along with the high-temperature induced modulus reduction and even viscous flow of the PI may cause the polymer chains in the neighborhood of the carbon dot to reorient. Polarized optical microscopy (POM) was applied to examine the states of polymer chain orientation for the PI films upon IR-DLWc process. With the polarizer (P) and analyzer (A) in crossed position, the POM optical micrographs for the sample fabricated at 1.8 W/40 msec were taken and shown in Fig. 6 a. As noted, the PI film itself is birefringent due to the preferred polymer chain orientation in the machine direction (MD) formed in the manufacturing process. When changing the angle between MD and the polarizer direction - Φ, we observe in Fig. 6a that the region in the neighborhood of the carbon dot and the background away from it have drastically different intensity variation patterns. As can be seen at Φ = 0°, there is a silent needle-shaped dark band in parallel to the MD direction and passing through the center of the carbon dot. With the coordinate system defined by the center of the carbon dot as the origin and the needle and its perpendicular direction as the axes, one can also see the presence of a bright lobe radiating in the 45° direction in each quadrant. When gradually increasing the angle Φ to 45°, the dark needle correspondingly appears brighter, and the bright lobe turns to darker. There is a reversal for this pattern change when the angle Φ further increases from 45° to 90°. On the basis of the POM results, we propose the orientation states of polymer chain around the carbon dot as shown in Fig. 6b. Far away from the carbon dot, the polymer chains take the orientation inherited from the parent PI film, pointing in the MD direction in general. Nearby the carbon dot, this uniaxial orientation state gradually transits to biaxially oriented with the polymer chains oriented along the circumferential direction of the carbon dot. The peculiar polymer chain orientation states as observed in Fig. 6a and proposed in Fig. 6b is believed to be a result of the interactions among multiple factors: along with the high-temperature induced modulus reduction and even viscous flow, the radial compression stress and the circumferential tensile stress created by the internal pressure of the trapped pyrolysis gas as well as the initial orientation states of the parent PI film and such induced anisotropy of the mechanical and viscoelastic properties. After the laser is turned off and when the temperature is cooling down below the glass transition temperature of the PI (∼420 °C), such an orientation state would be frozen to give the POM pattern as shown in Fig. 6a. Moreover, when we pierced through the dome structure to release the trapped pyrolysis gas and the associated pressure/stress, the POM pattern surrounding the carbon dot remains unchanged (Fig. S5), which further corroborates that polymer chain segment orientation is responsible for the observed birefringence pattern. The trapping of pyrolysis gas induced polymer chain reorientation is a unique observation to IR-DLWc process, which does not exist in the conventional DLWc as shown in Fig. S4.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In-situ tailoring the shape of carbon dots in IR-DLWc and its use for making sensitive piezoresistive sensors
ce:section.ce:para: As discussed earlier, the trapping of pyrolysis gas is able to create mechanical stresses to the PI matrix surrounding a carbon dot in IR-DLWc process. This unique behavior can be utilized for in-situ tailoring the shape of the carbon dot created by IR-DLWc. As demonstrated in Fig. 7 a, we first created a small-sized carbon dot - A at 1.8 W and 20 msec. At some distance (center-to-center) away, a large-sized carbon dot - B was then generated at 1.8 W and 40 msec. As shown in Fig. 7a, when this distance is less than ∼250 μm, the shape of carbon dot - A starts to change. With reducing the distance between A and B, the curvature of A at the side facing toward B gradually decreases or flattens. In the meanwhile, the shape of B shows no obvious change. This kind of shape changing behavior maintains even when A and B are connected to each other at a distance of ∼170 μm. However, when we reversed the creation order of the carbon dots, namely the large-sized first and then the small-sized, the shape change of the carbon dots manifest a totally different scenario. As shown in Fig. 7b, we created a large-sized carbon dot - C first at 1.8 W and 40 msec. Subsequently, with the same laser power, a small-sized carbon dot - D was then generated at a few discrete irradiation durations less than 40 msec. Within the selected experimental conditions, we can see in Fig. 7b that both the shape of C and D are changing, irrespective of the irradiation duration used for generating the small-sized carbon dot. Moreover, on the sides of C and D facing each other, the curvature of C decreases or flattens while the curvature of D increases and even it becomes a singular apex when the irradiation duration to create D is long enough to result in a connection of C and D.

The shape-change behavior of the carbon dots shown in Fig. 7a and 7b can be understood as follows. During the formation of the second carbon dot, the radial compression stress is generated due to the pyrolysis gas trapping effect. This compression stress would push the side of the first carbon dot that faces the second carbon dot away from it to result in a curvature decrease as shown in Fig. 7a. However, when the size of the second carbon dot is small enough, because of the stress concentration effect, the compression stress generated from it is sufficient to cause the failure of the PI bridge between these two carbon dots. As a consequence, the high-pressure hot pyrolysis gas in the second carbon dot rapidly channels through the PI bridge to the region occupied by the first carbon dot. The jet of the pyrolysis gas out of the second carbon dot thus causes the extensional deformation of the surrounding polymer matrix, which is then responsible for the local curvature decrease and even the singular apex formation as observed in Fig. 7b.

On the basis of the conventional DLWc method, we previously proposed a hierarchical contact design concept to fabricate highly-sensitive piezoresistive sensors for the use in small deformation sensing and measurement [6]. The key for the success of this concept is to construct a relatively weak contact between the basic structure units, e.g., dots. With this concept, a dot-dot-contact line sensor was fabricated by using the conventional DLWc, which possesses a gauge factor (GF = ΔR/R0ε – defined as the relative resistance change per unit strain, a quantity to describe the sensitivity of a piezoresistive sensor [49]) of ∼47, significantly higher than ∼3 for a plain line sensor created by the same method or by IR-DLWc [1]. Herein, we applied the same concept to fabricate a dot-dot-contact line sensor by using the IR-DLWc technique upon twice writing with the conditions of laser power 1.8 W, irradiation duration 40 msec and dot-to-dot distance 230 μm. Fig. 7c shows the optical micrograph of this dot-dot-contact line sensor; and its piezoresistive behavior – tensile strain-dependent relative resistance change is shown in Fig. 7d. It can be seen in Fig. 7d that the dot-dot-contact line sensor show reasonably good piezoresistive linearity and its GF evaluated to be ∼340, much higher than the similar dot-dot-contact line sensor fabricated by the conventional DLWc. The greatly improved GF for the dot-dot-contact line sensor fabricated by IR-DLWc is presumably attributed to its unique pyrolysis trapping effect and the correspondingly induced local deformation on the dot-dot contacts, which may help to reduce the contact strength to improve the sensor sensitivity. The durability of the dot-dot-contact line sensor fabricated by IR-DLWc was examined by submitting it to cyclic mechanical tests with the tensile strain periodically ramped from 0 to 0.2% at 0.0125 Hz for 200 cycles. The resistance change is shown in Fig. 7e. In the same figure, a few cycles of resistance change at the beginning and at the end of the test are shown for a better visualization. After 200 cycles, the initial resistance of the sensor decreased by about 7%; and its GF increased by 5%. Considering the sealing character, high piezoresistive sensitivity, and reasonable durability of the dot-dot-contact line sensor fabricated by IR-DLWc, we further explored its use as an ultrasonic sensor for underwater mapping of the ultrasound field of a bath sonication cleaner. In Fig. 7f, the picture of the ultrasonic sensor prototype and its voltage response are shown. Upon fast Fourier transform (FFT) treatment to the raw voltage response signals acquired by the ultrasonic sensor, we are able to map the ultrasound field of the bath sonication cleaner being tested. The results are also shown in Fig. 7f, which indicate that the ultrasound generated by the transducer is relatively uniform across its lateral dimension; and its intensity decays with increasing the distance from the transducer, as expected. The reasonably good piezoresistive performance of the dot-dot-contact line sensor fabricated by IR-DLWc and the successful demonstration for its use in ultrasound mapping suggest that the IR-DLWc technique may offer a versatile platform for developing multi-functional and multi-modal sensors. Certainly, in order to meet the strict requirements in practical applications, much work needs to be done in the future to optimize the IR-DLWc process for fabricating highly-sensitive and durable piezoresistive sensors.

In summary, with a combined modeling and experimental study, we investigated the processing-structure relationship of the direct laser writing carbonization technique based on an internal-reflection setup (IR-DLWc). The key laser processing conditions for in-situ sealing the carbon features in the interior of a polyimide film was identified; and their influence on the size of the carbon features were quantitatively established through an empirical correlation. Moreover, due to the trapping of the pyrolysis gas, the IR-DLWc process manifests a unique photothermal heating induced thermomechanical deformation phenomenon, which can be utilized for creating highly-sensitive piezoresistive sensors that are useful, e.g., for under water mapping of the ultrasound field. Considering the flexibility, versatility, simplicity of the IR-DLWc in creating electrically conductive carbon features sealed in-situ, as well as the unique photothermal heating induced thermomechanical phenomenon associated with this novel technique as identified in the present work, we expect IR-DLWc will find its promising applications in many different fields.

Zhufeng Jiang: Methodology, Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Jiangjiang Luo: Investigation, Resources. Jingwen Yao: Investigation, Resources. Chang Xu: Investigation, Resources. Aishuang Liu: Investigation, Resources. Yanbo Yao: Investigation, Writing – review & editing. Tao Liu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China [NSFC 22105141, NSFC51673140]; Startup funds provided by Soochow University [Q410900116]; State and Local Joint Engineering Laboratory for Novel Functional Polymeric Materials;and Priority Academic Program Development (PAPD) of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions.
The raw/processed data required to reproduce these findings cannot be shared at this time as the data also forms part of an ongoing study.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.matdes.2021.110162.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
